g.data-narration: Welcome to learning about instructions and instruction sequencing, the foundation of how computers execute programs.
Instructions are binary patterns that tell the processor what operation to perform and on what data.
g.text: A computer instruction is a binary pattern that specifies an operation to be performed.
Instructions tell the processor what to do and on which data, forming the bridge between software and hardware.

g.data-narration: Every instruction has two main parts: the opcode specifying the operation and operands indicating the data addresses.
g.text: Example: ADD R1, R2, R3 → Opcode: ADD | Operands: R1(dest), R2, R3

g.data-narration: A typical instruction format shows the opcode followed by one or more operand fields, defining how the instruction is structured.
There are six main types of instructions: data transfer, arithmetic, logic, shift/rotate, control, and I/O instructions.
g.text: Each type performs a specific category of operations on data.

g.data-narration: Data transfer instructions move data between registers, memory, and I/O. Arithmetic instructions perform mathematical operations.
g.text: Move data between registers, memory, I/O
• ADD R1, R2, R3 → R1 = R2 + R3
• SUB R1, R2, R3 → R1 = R2 - R3
• MUL R1, R2, R3 → R1 = R2 × R3
• DIV R1, R2, R3 → R1 = R2 ÷ R3

g.data-narration: Logic instructions perform bitwise operations. Shift and rotate instructions manipulate bits within registers.
g.text: • AND R1, R2, R3 → R1 = R2 AND R3
• OR R1, R2, R3 → R1 = R2 OR R3
• NOT R1, R2 → R1 = NOT R2
• XOR R1, R2, R3 → R1 = R2 XOR R3
• SHL R1, R2, 2 - Shift left by 2
• SHR R1, R2, 1 - Shift right by 1
• ROL R1, R2, 3 - Rotate left by 3
• ROR R1, R2, 1 - Rotate right by 1

g.data-narration: Control instructions alter execution sequence through branching. I/O instructions facilitate input and output operations.
g.text: • BNZ TARGET - Branch if not zero
• IN R1, PORT1 - Read from input port
• OUT PORT2, R1 - Write to output port
These enable communication with external devices
Used for keyboards, displays, sensors, etc.

g.data-narration: The fetch phase retrieves the instruction from memory using the address in the Program Counter, then increments the PC.
During decode, the opcode is interpreted. In execute, the ALU performs the operation and stores results.
Sequential execution fetches instructions from consecutive memory locations, with the PC automatically incrementing after each instruction.
g.text: Instructions execute one after another in order

g.data-narration: Branch instructions modify the PC to alter sequential flow. Conditional branches depend on status flags like zero or negative.
The Program Counter is a special register holding the address of the next instruction. It's modified by sequential, branch, and call operations.
The CALL instruction saves the return address and jumps to a subroutine. RETURN restores the saved address to continue execution.
After arithmetic operations, the processor sets condition flags: Zero, Negative, Carry, and Overflow for decision making.
g.text: Processor sets flags after arithmetic/logic operations:

g.data-narration: In summary, instructions are binary commands that processors execute through the fetch-decode-execute cycle, with the PC controlling the sequencing flow.
g.text: • Instructions = Opcode + Operands | 6 types: Data Transfer, Arithmetic, Logic, Shift/Rotate, Control, I/O
• Execution Cycle: Fetch → Decode → Execute → Store | PC controls sequencing
• Sequential: PC auto-increments | Branch: PC modified by condition | Subroutines: CALL/RETURN
